title: Simpson Tide

jokes.text: Homer Simpson! You stand accused of eating half the population of the Planet of the Doughnuts.
jokes.setup: Court scene with serious accusation format
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous crime of eating sentient doughnuts
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons absurdism - takes Homer's gluttony to cosmic proportions. Strong character integration with Homer's obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: You stand accused of the crime of eating sentient doughnuts. How do you plead?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not guilty! Your Honor, I didn't know they were sentient. I thought they were just really good at screaming.

jokes.text: Did you just take a bite out of me? / Um, maybe.
jokes.setup: Defense attorney trying to help Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer can't stop eating even his own lawyer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his compulsion overrides all reason. Economical dialogue with great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Doughnut Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Mr. Simpson, you need to stop eating and pay attention to your case!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But these donuts are so good... nom nom nom...

dialogue.speaker: Doughnut Defense Attorney
dialogue.line: Wait, why are you eating me?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... lawyer...

jokes.text: We place this last doughnut in the reactor core... exposing it to radiation, thereby making it big! Yeah, it worked on my hand!
jokes.setup: Need to share one doughnut among workers
jokes.punchline: Homer's solution reveals casual radiation exposure
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - nuclear safety treated casually. The hand reveal adds perfect visual punchline to the setup.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay guys, we've got one doughnut and five people. Here's what we do: we each take a bite, then whoever gets the radioactive bite gets the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Homer, that's insane. How would we even know who got the radioactive bite?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, whoever starts glowing, obviously.

jokes.text: It's my first day. / Since I've never seen you before, maybe it is your first day. / That's Homer Simpson. And he's been working here for 10 years.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to excuse incompetence with obvious lie
jokes.punchline: Burns is so out of touch he almost believes it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Burns' disconnect from reality makes Homer's transparent lie almost work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I can explain. I was actually working the whole time. I was just... invisible.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Invisible? By Jove, that's genius! Why didn't I think of that?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, he was asleep in the storage closet.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, don't be ridiculous. Obviously he was invisible. Give him a raise.

jokes.text: It's my first day. / Well, why didn't you sa- Oh, whoo! You're fired!
jokes.setup: Homer tries the same lie again
jokes.punchline: Burns catches himself being fooled twice
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure showing Burns isn't completely clueless, though not as strong as the original.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, my car broke down. Can I borrow one of your limousines?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Absolutely not, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But sir, my family is stranded on the highway!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... didn't you use that exact same story last week?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... no?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Fool me once, shame on you. Fool me twice... well, you're not going to fool me twice.

jokes.text: Blacula, followed by Blackenstein... and the Blunchblack of Blotre Blame.
jokes.setup: Parody of blaxploitation film names
jokes.punchline: Final title becomes complete nonsense alliteration
jokes.explanation: Clever escalation of blaxploitation parody into pure sound comedy. Low character integration as it's just background gag.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: Coming this summer: 'Blackula,' 'Blackenstein,' and 'Black Snake Moan'... wait, that's a real movie. Uh, 'Shaft in Africa,' 'Coffy,' and... 'Pimpadelic Superfly Mama Jama's Big Booty Bonanza!'

jokes.text: You're in the naval reserve: America's 17th line of defense... between the Mississippi National Guard and the League of Women Voters.
jokes.setup: Military recruitment should emphasize importance
jokes.punchline: Naval reserve is comically unimportant in hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with great escalation to absurd endpoint. Highly quotable despite low character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Reserve Narrator
dialogue.line: The Navy Reserve: We're not the Navy, but we play one on weekends.

jokes.text: And most of that time, you're drunk off your ass.
jokes.setup: Military recruitment promising easy duty
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of unprofessionalism
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery of subversive truth. Very economical but lacks character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Reserve Narrator
dialogue.line: Join the Naval Reserve, where we promise you'll never see combat.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Reserve Narrator
dialogue.line: You'll spend most of your time at home, doing absolutely nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Reserve Narrator
dialogue.line: And if we do call you in, we probably won't know what we're doing anyway.

jokes.text: Are you a homosex- / Oh, for God's sake, don't answer that. I could go to jail.
jokes.setup: Don't Ask Don't Tell policy reference
jokes.punchline: Recruiter panics at his own question
jokes.explanation: Topical reference to military policy creates cringe moment. Decent structure but somewhat dated.

dialogue.speaker: Navy Recruiter
dialogue.line: So, are you gay?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Navy Recruiter
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Navy!

jokes.text: We live in a highly technological age where fighting a war... is as simple as turning off a light. [Clapping] / We don't have a Clapper.
jokes.setup: Homer makes grand statement about modern warfare
jokes.punchline: He's literally talking about The Clapper device
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - sounds philosophical until the reveal. Perfect Homer logic and great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Modern warfare is all about remote control. You sit in a nice comfortable chair, push a button, and boom — your enemy is destroyed.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Clapper, Marge. Clap on, clap off. The Clapper.

jokes.text: Sorry, I can't hear you, Marge. I'm clapping. [Clapping Continues] Nighty-night.
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrates The Clapper
jokes.punchline: He keeps clapping to avoid hearing Marge's concerns
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Clapper bit. Very Homer behavior - using technology to avoid responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Clap on! Clap off! Clap on! Clap off!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we need to talk about your gambling debts—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Clap on! Clap off! Clap on! Clap off!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, please, this is serious—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Clap on! Clap off! Clap on! Clap off!

jokes.text: Gee, thanks, guys. This is just like The Deer Hunter.
jokes.setup: Friends joining Homer in naval reserve seems heartwarming
jokes.punchline: Reference to traumatic Vietnam War film
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy referencing tragic war film in cheerful context. Good economy but requires cultural knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Naval Reserve, men. I know this seems like a big step, but you're going to love it here.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: This is great, Homer! We're gonna be sailors!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Yeah, man. Living the dream.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just wait until you see the training videos. One of them is a real classic.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: What kind of classic?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's called 'Apocalypse Now.'

jokes.text: Apu runs away screaming in Vietnamese after Deer Hunter reference
jokes.setup: Homer's innocent Deer Hunter comment
jokes.punchline: Apu has PTSD flashback and flees
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the Deer Hunter reference with unexpected character backstory revelation - excellent visual comedy

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: No! No! The horror! The horror!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Why must you remind me of my troubled past?

jokes.text: I'll bring you a weapon of unimaginable destructive power! / Homer! / But only if you're good. Even if you're not.
jokes.setup: Homer promises to bring back military souvenirs
jokes.punchline: Escalates to WMDs, then undercuts his own condition
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's poor parenting instincts. The self-contradiction is very in-character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll bring back some cool military souvenirs from the base!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's stealing government property.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, come on, Marge. What's the worst that could happen? I'll just grab a few medals, maybe a helmet...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're gonna get in trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Trouble? I'll bring back a tank! No wait, a missile! A nuclear warhead! Weapons of mass destruction! I'll be the coolest dad in Springfield!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? Oh, but I'll be home by 5:30 for dinner.

jokes.text: Mine is! [Belches]
jokes.setup: Drill sergeant says mothers aren't there to help
jokes.punchline: Barney's mom is actually there
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barney moment - still dependent on his mother. Very economical but predictable setup.

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: Your mothers aren't here to help you!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Mine is.

dialogue.speaker: Barney's Mom
dialogue.line: That's right, pumpkin!

jokes.text: No way, man. My hair is who I am! / Ow, I'm a freak!
jokes.setup: Otto dramatically resists military haircut
jokes.punchline: Immediately caves when it's done
jokes.explanation: Standard resistance-to-acceptance pattern. Good for Otto's character but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: No way, man! I'm not getting a military haircut! My hair is like, totally part of my identity, dude.

dialogue.speaker: Barber
dialogue.line: It'll just take a few minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: I said no! You can't—

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa! Dude, that's awesome! I look so cool! Best haircut ever!

jokes.text: I don't like you, and you don't like me. / I like you. / You like me, but I don't like you. / Maybe you would like me if you got to know me.
jokes.setup: Drill sergeant tries to establish mutual antagonism
jokes.punchline: Homer's earnestness disrupts the formula
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of military movie tropes. Homer's genuine niceness confuses the hostile dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: Listen up, Simpson! I'm gonna break you down!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! When do we start? I'm so excited!

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: Wipe that smile off your face, maggot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're a great motivator, Sarge. Has anyone ever told you that?

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: I... what? No one's ever... just get out of my sight!

jokes.text: Well, I'm no Margaret Cho... but I do a pretty fair Columbo impression. [Imitating Columbo] Uh, one more thi- [Coughs] [Clears Throat] I should get a glass of water.
jokes.setup: Homer claims to be a comedian with celebrity comparison
jokes.punchline: His impression immediately fails due to dry throat
jokes.explanation: Good Homer overconfidence moment. Physical comedy element with the voice failure adds to the deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a comedian! I do impressions. I do a great impression of Cary Grant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *clears throat* Judy, Judy, Judy... *voice cracks* ...I'm thirsty.

jokes.text: Very nice, Simpson. But next time, tie the other end to the ship! [People Screaming]
jokes.setup: Homer successfully ties a knot
jokes.punchline: But he tied the ship to the dock instead of the rope to the ship
jokes.explanation: Classic setup-punchline with physical consequences. Good structure showing Homer's literal-mindedness.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Instructor
dialogue.line: Homer, you've successfully tied a knot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I tied the ship to the dock instead of the rope to the ship.

jokes.text: Congratulations! You are all pastry chefs! / Congratulations. You are all World War I historical re-creationists.
jokes.setup: Multiple graduation ceremonies happening simultaneously
jokes.punchline: Each more ridiculous than naval reserve
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation showing various levels of military seriousness. Lower character integration as background gag.

dialogue.speaker: French Officer
dialogue.line: Ah, ze French navy, ze most sophisticated in ze world!

dialogue.speaker: German Officer
dialogue.line: Bah! Ze German navy is superior!

dialogue.speaker: French Officer
dialogue.line: Your ships sink faster zan a baguette in water!

dialogue.speaker: German Officer
dialogue.line: At least we don't surrender at ze first sign of trouble!

dialogue.speaker: French Officer
dialogue.line: Zat is because you never see trouble coming, you fool!

jokes.text: Milhouse, my mom wears earrings. Do you think she's cool? / No! / I think she's hot! Sorry. It just slipped out.
jokes.setup: Bart questions Milhouse's earring by comparing to his mom
jokes.punchline: Milhouse accidentally reveals attraction to Marge
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy with the 'friend attracted to friend's mom' trope. Well-structured but familiar territory.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why are you wearing an earring? You look like my mom.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Your mom's hot!

jokes.text: That is so 1991.
jokes.setup: Bart does MC Hammer dance
jokes.punchline: Nelson dismisses it as passé with precise dating
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference with Nelson's unexpected cultural awareness. Very economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! That dance is so 1990. It's 1992 now.

jokes.text: Well, I'm a Gypsy. / Oh, really? Prove it. / [Imitating Dracula] 'I vant to suck your blood!' / Nuh-uh. That's a vampire. But, uh, they're also covered.
jokes.setup: Milhouse claims Gypsy heritage to keep earring
jokes.punchline: Does vampire impression instead, Skinner inexplicably accepts it
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic with Skinner's bureaucratic flexibility. The vampire/gypsy confusion is perfectly silly.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: I'm part Gypsy, so I can wear an earring.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: That's not school policy, Van Houten.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: *does vampire impression* I vant to suck your blood!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, that's a legitimate cultural expression. Earring approved.

jokes.text: I lost this eye in Haiti. I was drinkin a mai tai... and I forgot to take the little parasol out.
jokes.setup: Expected dramatic war story about losing an eye
jokes.punchline: Mundane cocktail accident
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. Subverts war story expectations beautifully. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: I lost me eye in a terrible accident at sea. We were in the midst of a horrible storm, waves crashin' over the deck, lightning strikin' all around us...

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: And then a bottle of mai tai came flyin' through the air and hit me right in the face.

jokes.text: I was on P.T. 109 with John F. Kennedy. I was the first to discover his terrible secret. / Ich bin ein Berliner. / He's a Nazi! Get 'im!
jokes.setup: Grampa promises to reveal JFK's secret
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets famous Berlin speech as Nazi confession
jokes.explanation: Brilliant historical comedy mixing real events with absurd misunderstanding. Great Grampa character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I got a secret about JFK that'll blow your mind!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: He told me what 'Ich bin ein Berliner' really means.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: It's German for 'I am a Nazi!'

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: JFK was a secret Nazi the whole time!

jokes.text: And hit me over the head with a hat? That's not funny. That hurts! [Laughing] / Stop laughing at me!
jokes.setup: Gilligan complains about people hitting him with hats
jokes.punchline: People immediately laugh at his pain
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy about sitcom violence. Decent structure but relies on reference humor.

dialogue.speaker: Gilligan
dialogue.line: Why does everyone keep hitting me with their hats?

dialogue.speaker: Others
dialogue.line: *laugh at his pain*

jokes.text: An earring. How rebellious. In a conformist sort of way.
jokes.setup: Bart shows off his new earring
jokes.punchline: Lisa points out the paradox of trendy rebellion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa observation - intelligent, succinct, and cutting. Great economic phrasing that's highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Check out my new earring. I'm a rebel.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, you bought that earring at the mall. The same mall where everyone else buys their earrings.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're not rebelling against society — you're shopping at it.

jokes.text: Milhouse jumped off a cliff? I'm there.
jokes.setup: Homer uses 'jump off a cliff' as hypothetical deterrent
jokes.punchline: Bart takes it literally and wants to join
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of parental rhetoric. Perfect Bart response showing his reckless nature.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: If all your friends jumped off a cliff, would you do it?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: If they were all jumping off a cliff, I'd have to be there.

jokes.text: My neck hurts, and my ear hurts. I have two owies.
jokes.setup: Everyone has earrings now
jokes.punchline: Ralph describes pain in childish terms
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - innocent and slightly dim. Good character integration but simple humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My ears hurt!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: It's like there's little ouchies in my ear holes.

jokes.text: 'Nuc-u-lar'! It's pronounced, 'nuc-u-lar.' / Oh, whatever. / 'Nuc-u-lar.'
jokes.setup: Officer says 'nuclear' correctly
jokes.punchline: Homer corrects him with wrong pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer moment showing his confident ignorance. Became a real-world reference point for mispronunciation. Excellent character work.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: nuclear

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's pronounced 'nucular'

jokes.text: I'm a man of few words. Any questions? / Uh, is the poop deck really what I think it is? / I like the cut of your jib. / What's a jib? / Promote that man.
jokes.setup: Captain claims to be man of few words
jokes.punchline: Homer's innocent questions lead to promotion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Homer's naivety can be mistaken for wisdom. Well-structured sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Captain Tennille
dialogue.line: I'm a man of few words.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is that why you never talk to me?

dialogue.speaker: Captain Tennille
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, could you talk to me more often?

dialogue.speaker: Captain Tennille
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, do you think I could get a promotion?

dialogue.speaker: Captain Tennille
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Love Boat theme song as 'ancient sea chanty'
jokes.setup: Captain introducing traditional naval song
jokes.punchline: It's actually 1970s TV theme song
jokes.explanation: Clever anachronism humor with good setup but extended musical sequence dilutes impact

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: Now, lads, I'll teach you a traditional naval song that's been passed down for generations!

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: 🎵 Come and listen to a story about a man named Jed, a poor mountaineer barely kept his family fed... 🎵

dialogue.speaker: Crew
dialogue.line: That's not a naval song!

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: Well, it was on a boat once.

jokes.text: I want peas. / We all want peace! But it's always just out of reach. / With a knife! / Exactly! Not with the olive branch, but the bayonet.
jokes.setup: Captain asks what Homer wants from life
jokes.punchline: Homer means vegetables, Captain hears philosophy about peace/war
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay with perfect character dynamics. Homer's literalness creates accidental profundity that impresses the Captain.

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: What do you want from life?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, the finer things. A nice house, good food, beautiful vegetables.

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: Ah yes, vegetables. The ultimate symbol of man's struggle between the desire for peace and the inevitability of war.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, sure.

jokes.text: You're like the son I never had. / And you're like the father I never visit.
jokes.setup: Captain expresses paternal affection
jokes.punchline: Homer's response reveals family dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response that's both touching and revealing of his relationship with Grampa. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Captain McCallister
dialogue.line: You know, you remind me of my own boy. I love you, son.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I love you too, Captain. Say, you wouldn't happen to have any beer on you, would ya?

jokes.text: You gotta poke 'im with a stick. / I don't want to go to school today! [Snores]
jokes.setup: Barney gives parenting advice to wake someone up
jokes.punchline: His own son responds like he's being woken for school
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing dysfunctional family dynamics. Reveals Barney's parenting through his advice.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: The key to waking someone up is a firm hand and a loud voice. You gotta be authoritative.

dialogue.speaker: Barney's son
dialogue.line: Five more minutes, Dad! I don't wanna go to school!

jokes.text: You gotta poke 'im with a stick [to wake Barney in torpedo tube]
jokes.setup: Officer trying to communicate with obstruction in torpedo tube
jokes.punchline: Homer casually suggests treating Barney like an animal
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Homer's casual treatment of Barney's alcoholism

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: We've got an obstruction in the torpedo tube!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Have you tried treating it like an animal? Just make some noise and see if it comes out.

jokes.text: What do we do? / I don't know. / Fifteen seconds to collision. We need a decision. / What would the captain say in my spot? / Don't fire the torpedoes! / Fire the torpedoes!
jokes.setup: Homer must make critical military decision
jokes.punchline: Takes Captain's advice opposite to what he intended
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of tension with Homer's characteristic misunderstanding. Sets up major plot point.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Captain, what should I do?

dialogue.speaker: Captain
dialogue.line: Whatever you do, don't press that button.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Good advice. I'll press it.

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: Sir, that's the nuclear launch sequence!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Too late!

jokes.text: Fire! Not me, a torpedo!
jokes.setup: Homer gives fire command
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he means weapon, not himself
jokes.explanation: Quick clarification joke showing Homer's awareness of potential confusion. Good economy but simple.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fire!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not me, you idiot! Fire the weapon!

jokes.text: You just shot the captain out of the torpedo tube. / That means you're in charge, Homer. / I am?
jokes.setup: Homer's mistake has consequences
jokes.punchline: Homer is surprised to be promoted through elimination
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's incompetence into leadership. Sets up further comedy but not exceptional itself.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm the supervisor now? But I didn't even apply!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, Homer. Everyone else got fired.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm a supervisor!

jokes.text: Ice-blended mocha drinks and David Schwimmer. / Yes, he is handsome in an ugly sort of way.
jokes.setup: Damage report asks what's working
jokes.punchline: Random modern amenities, Homer agrees about Schwimmer
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor with 90s pop culture reference. The Schwimmer comment is oddly specific but dated.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Well, the jukebox works, the neon sign works, the toilet's still broken...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Schwimmer!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: David Schwimmer. He's still working.

jokes.text: Mr. Sulu, make a left! / Aye, aye, Captain. Setting course for Rigel Seven. I mean home.
jokes.setup: Homer uses Star Trek reference for navigation
jokes.punchline: Moe plays along with sci-fi before correcting himself
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference humor with Moe getting caught up in the fantasy. Decent but relies on recognition.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Set a course for the stars, Moe. Full impulse power!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Aye aye, Captain. I'm locking in coordinates to the Neutral Zone. Shields up, raise the warp core temperature...

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Wait, what am I saying? You want another beer?

jokes.text: My Homer is not a Communist. He may be a liar, a pig, an idiot, a Communist- But he is not a porn star!
jokes.setup: Grampa defends Homer against accusations
jokes.punchline: Lists terrible qualities including the thing he's denying, then denies something else
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons joke structure - defense becomes attack, then misdirects to random topic. Perfect Grampa character moment and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: My son may be a liar, a pig, an idiot, a communist, but he is not a porn star!

jokes.text: The Soviet Union? I thought you guys broke up. / [Laughing] Yes! That's what we wanted you to think! [Evil laughing]
jokes.setup: Russians appear despite USSR collapse
jokes.punchline: Reveals Cold War paranoia fantasy that USSR faked breakup
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire playing on Cold War conspiracy theories. Very quotable and original concept.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me the Soviet Union never actually collapsed?

dialogue.speaker: Russian
dialogue.line: Is all elaborate hoax, comrade. We fake dissolution to make you Americans lower guard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! I knew it! I KNEW IT! This whole time I thought we won!

dialogue.speaker: Russian
dialogue.line: You think Cold War is over? Cold War never over. Is still very cold.

jokes.text: and Sherri, but not Terri. / Ha-ha!
jokes.setup: Principal lists who gets saved in nuclear war
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary twin discrimination, Nelson laughs at exclusion
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about arbitrary selection criteria. Nelson's reaction adds perfect cruelty element.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: In the event of a nuclear war, we will save only the most valuable members of society: the talented, the beautiful, the rich...

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Nelson, I'm afraid you won't be on the list.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha, Rod and Todd are going to die!

jokes.text: It's filling up with a clear, nonalcoholic liquid. / You mean, water? / Yeah, that's it.
jokes.setup: Emergency report with technical description
jokes.punchline: Can't identify basic substance water
jokes.explanation: Good character-based humor showing naval incompetence. Simple but effective structure.

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: We've got a hull breach! Water's pouring in at 200 gallons per minute!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Water? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Sailor
dialogue.line: It's a clear liquid, makes up 71% of Earth's surface...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Never heard of it.

jokes.text: [Baby talking] Oh, Mr. Snookums. Oh, I 'wuv' you so- What are you looking at?
jokes.setup: Thinking of loved ones in crisis moment
jokes.punchline: Moe's pathetic attachment to inanimate object
jokes.explanation: Great character moment revealing Moe's loneliness with perfect embarrassment and defensiveness

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: In a crisis moment, a man thinks of his loved ones. Well, I got my bar stool.

jokes.text: I'll plug your hole! I mean, eureka!
jokes.setup: Bart suggests earring could plug leak
jokes.punchline: Homer's response sounds inappropriate, he quickly recovers
jokes.explanation: Accidental innuendo humor that's quickly corrected. Somewhat cheap but Homer's embarrassment makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what she said! I mean... the boat. The boat, Bart.

jokes.text: Uh, it's my first day!
jokes.setup: Homer must explain his actions to military tribunal
jokes.punchline: Uses the same excuse that got him fired
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that shows Homer's consistency and everyone's reaction shows it worked this time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you see, I was just doing my job. I didn't think anyone would find out.

dialogue.speaker: Military Officer
dialogue.line: That's the same excuse you gave when you were fired from the nuclear plant!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it worked then!

jokes.text: Uh, it's my first day!
jokes.setup: Homer facing consequences for submarine disaster
jokes.punchline: Returns to episode-opening excuse in absurd context
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to opening showing Homer's consistent character - same excuse for completely different disaster

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just trying to... wait, what was I doing again?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: You sank a submarine, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, that's right. But I was late for work at the nuclear plant!

jokes.text: I would throw the book at you, but I've been indicted in the Tailhook scandal
jokes.setup: Officer preparing to punish Homer
jokes.punchline: Can't punish due to own real-world scandal
jokes.explanation: Good topical humor referencing real military scandal with appropriate dark tone

dialogue.speaker: Naval Officer
dialogue.line: I'm going to have to punish you for your insubordination, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Naval Officer
dialogue.line: However, I've recently been accused of some... indiscretions myself, so I'm in no position to judge. You're free to go.

jokes.text: You can't spell 'dishonorable' without 'honorable.'
jokes.setup: Homer receives dishonorable discharge
jokes.punchline: Finds positive spin through literal word construction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic finding silver lining through word manipulation. Excellent economy and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dishonorable discharge? Wait a minute... dis-honorable. That's a compliment! I'm dis-honorable, which means I'm very honorable!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not how words work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, I'm also un-intelligent, non-handsome, and in-continent!

jokes.text: Then can I get a tattoo that says, 'Bite me'? / You never know when it might come in handy.
jokes.setup: Bart asks for inappropriate tattoo
jokes.punchline: Homer considers practical applications
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist parenting moment with Homer's twisted logic. Decent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, can I get a tattoo?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Absolutely not, Bart. You're way too young.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, I want one that says 'Mom'.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, that's actually not a bad idea. It shows respect. But what if you meet another woman? You'll have to get 'Mom' tattooed on your other arm, then your chest, then your butt...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I could use you as a filing system!

jokes.text: Weapons-grade plutonium? / Ask your mother.
jokes.setup: Bart's requests get increasingly dangerous
jokes.punchline: Homer passes ultimate parenting decision to Marge
jokes.explanation: Good escalation to absurd parenting territory. Homer's avoidance technique is very in-character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, can I have a BB gun?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Can I go bungee jumping?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Can I join the Navy SEALS?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Can I play with dynamite?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ask your mother.

tmdb_overview: When Homer joins the Naval Reserve, a captain takes a liking to him and leaves him in charge of a nuclear submarine.
insight: Naval satire hits 83.9 with 53 jokes in 22 minutes—Bart's cadet arc drives character comedy throughout.
